food__drink_hero_application.box_1_the_food_drink_hero_question_600_w:
I am applying as the owner-chef of The Green Larder Bistro in Retford, where I have spent the last four and a half years building a small, neighbourhood-focused business around three principles: seasonality, sustainability, and community. I grew up in Worksop; my dad was a butcher on Bridge Street and my mum was a dinner lady at a local primary school. Food, for us, was always about looking after people, using what was to hand, and wasting as little as possible. When I opened The Green Larder in May 2021, I wanted to bring that same ethos into a modern, approachable bistro that felt genuinely rooted in North Notts.

We change our menu every four to six weeks, and every single dish starts with what is available from local suppliers. Our vegetables come from a smallholding just outside Clarborough (run by the Wilson family), our flour from Tuxford Windmill, our cheeses from a dairy in Sutton-on-Trent, and all of our meat from independent butchers within a 15‑mile radius. We have a strict policy that at least 80% of our ingredients by value must be sourced from within the East Midlands, and we track this weekly. I personally visit each farm or producer before we add them to our supplier list, and we pay a fair, agreed price that allows them to farm with better welfare and environmental standards.

We have built a reputation for being "fanatical" about reducing food waste. We run a nose-to-tail, root-to-leaf kitchen: cauliflower leaves become a smoky side dish; carrot tops are turned into pesto; beef trim is used in staff meals or croquettes on the bar menu. We compost all vegetable offcuts in partnership with a local community garden project, and we’ve reduced our general waste by 65% since opening. Our fryer oil is collected and converted to biofuel, and we eliminated single-use plastic takeaway containers in 2022, moving to fully compostable packaging.

The heart of The Green Larder, however, is our community work. During the COVID-19 lockdowns, before we even officially opened, I adapted the space into a community kitchen. With the help of volunteers, we cooked and delivered over 5,000 hot meals to shielding residents and key workers across Retford and surrounding villages. I personally coordinated the logistics, phoned isolated residents to check dietary needs, and often did late-night deliveries after finishing prep for the next day. Even now, we maintain a quiet "pay it forward" scheme: regulars can add £5 to their bill which goes into a fund for free meals for anyone who needs one, no questions asked. On average, we provide 25–30 such meals a week.

I also run a free monthly "Cooking on a Budget" workshop in our dining room on Monday evenings, aimed at families and individuals referred by local schools, social prescribers, and Bassetlaw Food Bank. We focus on practical skills: how to make a chicken stretch to three meals, how to batch-cook soups and stews, how to use cheaper cuts of meat and store cupboard staples without sacrificing nutrition or flavour. We send people home with printed recipes, a bag of ingredients, and my email address so they can ask follow-up questions.

Ethics and staff wellbeing are central to what we do. All 11 members of my team are on permanent contracts with the Real Living Wage as a minimum, with paid breaks and a share of tips distributed transparently. We operate a 4‑day week rota for full-time staff to protect work-life balance, and we provide free, hot staff meals twice a day using surplus ingredients. I mentor two young chefs from North Notts College, offering structured placements that include not just cooking, but also supplier visits, costings, menu design, and an introduction to sustainable kitchen practices.

I believe I am a Food & Drink Hero not because we are the fanciest restaurant, but because we are stubbornly committed to doing the right thing in all the small, everyday ways that add up: paying farmers fairly, feeding people who are struggling, training the next generation, and treating food as a means of connection rather than just a product. My hope is that The Green Larder shows that a small independent business in North Notts can be both commercially viable and genuinely values‑driven, and that our example might encourage others to adopt similar practices.
food__drink_hero_application.box_2_supporting_information:
Professional background and qualifications:
- Level 3 NVQ Diploma in Professional Cookery – North Nottinghamshire College (2011)
- Level 3 Award in Supervising Food Safety in Catering (Highfield) – renewed 2024
- City & Guilds Level 2 Award in Sustainability in Professional Kitchens (2022)
- Completed a 2‑year apprenticeship at a 2 Rosette restaurant in Nottingham (2012–2014)

Awards and recognitions:
- "Best New Food & Drink Business" – Retford Business Awards 2022
- Finalist, "Sustainable Business of the Year" – East Midlands Hospitality Awards 2023
- TripAdvisor Traveller’s Choice Award 2023 and 2024 (top 10% of restaurants worldwide based on reviews)
- Featured as "One to Watch" in the Nottinghamshire Food & Drink Guide 2023 print edition

Press and media coverage:
- Article in the Retford Times (March 2023), "The Bistro Feeding Retford in More Ways Than One" – focused on our pay‑it‑forward meals and budget cooking classes.
- Interview on BBC Radio Nottingham (June 2024) about rising food costs and how small restaurants can support local suppliers while keeping menus accessible.
- Short feature in the online blog "Eat East Mids" (September 2022) highlighting our zero‑waste approach to menu design.

Community and education work:
- Monthly free "Cooking on a Budget" classes since October 2021 – around 30–35 participants per session. We estimate over 700 individuals have attended at least one session.
- Quarterly "Meet the Producer" evenings where local farmers, cheesemakers, and millers come to the bistro to talk to guests about their work. These events often sell out within 48 hours and help build direct relationships between customers and producers.
- Ongoing collaboration with Bassetlaw Food Bank: surplus bread, soups, and stews are donated twice a week, and I provide recipe cards tailored to the typical contents of their food parcels.

Sustainability and sourcing details:
- Electricity from 100% renewable sources since 2022 via a green energy supplier.
- Food waste reduced by 65% (by weight) between 2021 and 2024; measured by weekly bin audits done by me and my sous-chef.
- Approximately 82–86% of our ingredients (by spend) are sourced from within the East Midlands; 60% from within a 25‑mile radius of Retford.
- We print our menus on recycled paper and encourage guests to use the digital menu via QR code when possible.

Staff development and mentoring:
- Formal partnership with North Notts College catering department to take 2–3 students per year on structured placements.
- Internal training sessions every six weeks covering topics such as allergen management, mental health awareness in hospitality, and low‑waste cooking techniques.
- One of our former apprentices, Jake Thompson, has now progressed to junior sous-chef and leads Saturday lunch service independently.

Online and social proof:
- Average rating of 4.8/5 on Google (based on ~320 reviews as of November 2024).
- Average rating of 5.0/5 on Facebook (based on ~190 recommendations).
- Instagram following of around 3,400, where we regularly share behind‑the‑scenes videos of prep, supplier visits, and staff stories to demystify restaurant work and highlight our local partners.

I am including these details to demonstrate that our ethos is embedded across all aspects of The Green Larder – from the food on the plate and how it is sourced, to the way we treat our staff and neighbours, and the way we represent North Notts food culture online and in the press.
